publicationstmt.publisher: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday February 20, 2026 at 18:00

corpname.part: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

did.abstract: The collection contains photographic negatives that document Edward McKnight Kauffer's work up to 1939 and cover a broad array of topics and uses. These uses include reproductions of posters McKnight Kauffer created in the 1910s and 1920s, images of exhibitions held in the gallery attached to Percy Lund, Humphries and Co., samples of designer Marion Dorn's work, and product images that were used in McKnight Kauffer's design work during the 1930s.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection contains photographic negatives that document Edward McKnight Kauffer's work up to 1939 and cover a broad array of topics and uses. These uses include reproductions of posters McKnight Kauffer created in the 1910s and 1920s, images of exhibitions held in the gallery attached to Percy Lund, Humphries and Co., samples of designer Marion Dorn's work, and product images that were used in McKnight Kauffer's design work during the 1930s.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The collection is the physical property of the Yale Center for British Art. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, contact the Yale Center for British Art Archives.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. All material has been digitized. Researchers must consult the digital surrogates instead of the originals. For further information consult the Yale Center for British Art Archives.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Yale Center for British Art, Gift of Henry S. Hacker, Yale BA 1965

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Edward McKnight Kauffer Photographic Negatives, Yale Center for British Art, Gift of Henry S. Hacker, Yale BA 1965

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The series is arranged into ten series: Series I. Books and magazines; Series II. Exhibitions; Series III. Logos and logo design; Series IV. Nudes; Series V. Portraits; Series VI. Posters; Series VII. Product samples; Series VIII. Set designs; Series IX. Subject files; Series X. Works by Marion Dorn.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Edward McKnight Kauffer (1890 December 14 - 1954 October 22) was a graphic designer active in both England and the United States. Although best known for his poster art, he was an innovative designer whose work also included book covers and illustrations, paintings, and set design.

Born in Great Falls, Montana, Kauffer spent his childhood in Evansville, Illinois. By 1904, he was painting sets at the Evansville Opera House, then joined a traveling theater company where he continued scene painting. Moving across country with the theatre company, Kauffer eventually landed in San Fransico, California. Rather than continue traveling, Kauffer began to work at Paul Elder and Company bookstore. While living in San Fransico, Kauffer took classes at the Mark Hopkins Institute of Art, and was further encouraged by Paul Elder himself, who allowed Kauffer to have the first exhibition of his own art in the bookstore's gallery space. Edward Kauffer's work caught the attention of Professor Joseph McKnight. McKnight provided funding for Kauffer to further his artistic study. This patronage would lead Kauffer to add McKnight's name into his own.

Beginning in 1912 and continuing to 1913, McKnight Kauffer took courses at the Art Institute of Chicago. During his time, he attended the International Exhibition of Modern Art, known as the Armory Show. The Armory Show included Cubist, Impressionist, and Fauvist works by artists such as Duchamp, Picasso and others, marking the first time large groups of Americans encountered modern European art. The impact of the Armory Show has cast a long shadow over American art, encouraging American artists to experiment with abstraction, subject matter, and material.

Following his time in Chicago, McKnight Kauffer made his way to Baltimore, then to Algiers, northward through Italy and Germany, and then France where he continued to study and paint. While in Paris, McKnight Kauffer met his first wife, Grace Ehrlich, an American pianist also studying in the city at the time. The two married on 1914 July 7. They remained in Paris until the outbreak of the First World War in 1914. The couple moved to England instead of returning to America due to insufficient funds for the journey.

McKnight Kauffer's first major commission came in 1915 by way of Frank Pick, publicity manager for the Underground Electric Railways Company of London. Prick requested two posters that highlighted rural destinations that could be reached through the Underground. They were remarkably successful, and Kauffer would go on to design dozens more for the London Underground. These posters reflected Kauffer's personal landscape work influenced by French painters from the south of France, Japanese prints, and the German poster tradition.

During the First World War, McKnight Kauffer also exhibited his work, and became involved with several artistic group endeavors. This included the London Group, for whom he designed graphics and promotional material. Working with a non-corporate client allowed for greater experimentation. Following the war's conclusion, Kauffer created set designs, costumes, and promotional material for the Art League of Service, a traveling theater company that performed in rural parts of England.

The 1920s saw McKnight Kauffer continue to make posters. In addition to his enduring relationship with the London Underground, McKnight Kauffer created advertisements for Shell Oil, Eno's Fruit Salt, and Eastman and Son – a dry cleaning firm who in response to public interest to McKnight Kauffer's work, created display signage that read "A New McKnight Kauffer Poster Will Appear Here Shortly." In 1927 he created film posters for Alfred Hitchcock's The Lodger and Fritz Lang's Metropolis , both of which were rejected for being "too good." Towards the end of the 1920s, he also began to experiment with incorporating photographs into poster advertisements, which would become a regular part of McKnight Kauffer's poster work in the 1930s through 1950s.

McKnight Kauffer's work in book design also began in the 1920s and would remain a steady part of his practice for the rest of his career. He created covers and illustrations for works by individuals such as T.S. Eliot, and worked with publishers and presses of all sizes. Beyond covers, McKnight Kauffer created fully illustrated books such as a 1925 reprinted edition of Robert Burton's The Anatomy of Melancholy (London: Nonesuch Press, 1925) and a reprint of Don Quixote (London: Nonesuch Press, 1930.) Some of his book work was done for friends, as by the 1920s McKnight Kauffer's social circle included Aldous Huxley, T.S. Eliot, Virgina and Leonard Woolf, as well as his partner and future second wife, Marion Dorn. Dorn herself was a trained fine artist who would become a celebrated interior designer. She and McKnight Kauffer collaborated on textiles together - especially carpeting - and often shared the same clients such as Fortnum and Mason and the London Underground.

Percy Lund, Humphries and Co., a book publisher out of London and Bradford, England, hired McKnight Kauffer to be their creative director in 1930. While there, he created book jackets, illustrations, and promotional material for the firm. Working out of a studio shared with Man Ray and Francis Bruguiere at 12 Bedford Square, London, England, McKnight Kauffer organized small shows in the building's exhibition space. These shows featured works by Man Ray himself, Jan Tschichold, and Rudolf Koch.

The late 1930s saw McKnight Kauffer's career slow down. Fewer commissions came in, although he had a successful showing of his work in New York City at the Museum of Modern Art in 1937. He and Marion Dorn left London in 1938 for a leased home in Northend, Buckinghamshire until 1940 and the outbreak of the Second World War. As they were both Americans, the couple was ordered by the United States Embassay to return. They landed in New York City.

Returning to America and finding work was not easy for McKnight Kauffer. Advertisers in the United States were focused on graphics and text, which was in sharp contrast to McKnight Kauffer's work which by this time frequently engaged with abstraction and works that allowed room for interpretation. Some agencies embraced Kauffer's approach, and he built up a group of clients. In addition to creating posters for the New York City subway system, he did work for the Ringling Brothers Barnum and Bailey Circus, as well as American Airlines. Book covers continued to bring in a steady flow of income, and included one of his most iconic works, the cover of Ralph Ellison's Invisible Man (New York: Random House, 1952). Covers also permitted McKnight Kauffer to experiment with typography at this time, such as his covers for Aldous Huxley's Brave New World (New York: Harper and Brothers, 1946) and Ulysses by James Joyce (New York: Random House, 1946).

In 1950, Marion Dorne and Edward McKnight Kauffer officially wed at City Hall in New York City. He continued to work for clients such as the Modern Library to create book covers and PanAmerican Airlines for travel posters. He died on 1954 October 22.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains images that feature McKnight Kauffer's book covers and illustrations, as well as magazine covers. Also included are images of other books made by other presses, possibly representing reference images for book design.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: De Bono, Emilio . Anno XIIII; the Conquest of an Empire , by Emilio De Bono; with an Introduction by Benito Mussolini. London: Cresset Press, 1937.

did.unittitle: The Daily Telegraph Map of Abyssinia
Cresset Press, Bright Ghost ; Anatomy of Frustration

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Braddock, Joseph. Bright Ghost: Recollections of a Georgian Boyhood. London: The Cresset Press, 1936.
Wells H.G.. The Anatomy of Frustration; a modern synthesis. London: The Cresset Press, 1936.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Milton, John, 1608-1674. Paradise Lost. London: The Cresset Press, 1931.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Miguel de Cervantes. Don Quixote de La Mancha. London: Nonesuch Press, 1930.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bennett, Arnold. Elsie and the Child. London: Cassell and Company Ltd., 1929.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reynolds, Mary. Maison de santé. Paris: Éditions Briant-Robert, 1926

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Defoe, Daniel. The life and strange surprizing adventures of Robinson Crusoe of York Mariner. London: Frederick Etchells and Hugh Macdonald, 1929.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Birkenhead F. E. Smith, Earl of. The World in 2030 A.D. London: Hodder and Stoughton, 1930.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains images of exhibition space and objects. They include the exhibition space at Percy Lund, Humpries and Co.'s 12 Bedfore Square, London address, which Kauffer used during his time as artistic director for the firm.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.

Series III. Logos and logo design

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series features logos and similar corporate identity images that McKnight Kauffer created or otherwise relied upon for use in his advertising work.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Negatives in this series contain nude photographs of women.

did.unittitle: Eye looking through bird shaped hole
Statue of Liberty and hand holding a book

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Negatives in this series contain individual portraits. Included are several portraits of Man Ray, who shared studio space with Kauffer during Kauffer's time as artistic director at Percy Lund and Humphries Co..

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The negatives in this series represent reproductions of many of McKnight Kauffer's best known poster designs for clients including Shell, the General Post Office, and the London Underground.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series features images of product samples. The images include both reference material as well as McKnight Kauffer's posters.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Negatives in this series are reproductions of set design pieces that McKnight Kauffer created and were likely meant to serve as reference files.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Negatives in this series include images of hotel interiors and hotel advertisement booklets, models associated with specific clothing brands, and negatives with the names of corporate entities that appear to show advertisements. It is possible that some of the hotel interior images are associated with Marion Dorn, whose interior work included clients such as the Savoy.

Series X. Works by Marion Dorn

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Marion Dorn was a textile designer active in the 1930s through 1960s, as well as McKnight Kauffer's long time partner. The negatives in this series features samples of her curtain and fabric work.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by title. Where possible, original titles and headings have been retained.
